content: session-factory.ts was the primary motivation for the entire refactor. The 60-line session creation block inside spawn() was extracted into createAgentSessionFromDefinition() specifically so the parallel-agent-spawning plan can reuse it for multi-turn sessions without duplicating setup logic. The other extractions (model-resolution, definition-resolution) were opportunistic cleanup made possible by the same pass.

When splitting a monolithic orchestration file, extract pure-function leaf modules first (model-resolution.ts, definition-resolution.ts, rendering.ts, authorization.ts), then extract the stateful/compositional pieces (session-factory.ts, chain-tool.ts, spawn-tool.ts) that depend on them, and slim the entry point last. This ordering avoids temporary circular dependencies during the refactor.

When extracting modules from a file that has external consumers, add re-exports in the original file (export * from './new-module.ts') to preserve the public API without breaking import paths. agent-spawner.ts re-exports everything from model-resolution.ts, definition-resolution.ts, and session-factory.ts so callers importing from agent-spawner.ts continue to work unchanged.

Dependency direction in lib/orchestration/ is strictly linear: model-resolution.ts and definition-resolution.ts are pure leaf modules with no internal deps → session-factory.ts imports from both → agent-spawner.ts imports from session-factory.ts. chain-runner.ts imports directly from model-resolution.ts (not through agent-spawner.ts). No cycles.

Pi extension tools are registered via register*(pi, getRuntime) functions: registerChainTool(pi, getRuntime) in chain-tool.ts and registerSpawnTool(pi, getRuntime) in spawn-tool.ts. Each tool module is self-contained (imports its own rendering and authorization helpers) and exports only the registration function. index.ts calls them in order, keeping it at ~30 lines.

Tool registration order in the orchestration extension must be preserved: registerChainTool() must be called before registerSpawnTool() in index.ts. Reversing this order changes the order tools appear in agent context, which can affect behavior. This constraint is invisible from the tool implementations themselves — it is only documented in the plan and enforced by the index.ts call order.

When TASK-097 ran, definition-resolution.ts already existed on disk from a prior incomplete refactoring but agent-spawner.ts still had inline duplicates of those functions. The worker had to reconcile both — removing duplicates and rewriting agent-spawner.ts cleanly — rather than doing a simple extraction. Large files in active development accumulate partial extractions that must be reconciled before a clean split is possible.

isSubagentAllowed() was extracted to its own authorization.ts module despite being a single small function. The decision was made because authorization is a distinct security-policy concern that should be independently readable, testable, and replaceable — not because of size. Even a 15-line function warrants its own module when it represents a separate conceptual responsibility.

No new test files were added during this refactor. Tests remain in tests/orchestration/agent-spawner.test.ts and tests/orchestration/agent-spawner.spawn.test.ts and test behavior through the public API (agent-spawner.ts re-exports), not through internal module paths. Trade-off: internal modules like model-resolution.ts and session-factory.ts are not independently tested. Accepted because this is a pure structural refactor with no behavior changes — existing tests provide sufficient coverage through the re-export surface.

Task AC checkboxes (inside AC:BEGIN/AC:END blocks) cannot be ticked using task_edit — the tool does not support checkbox toggling. Workers must edit the raw markdown file directly, replacing '- [ ]' with '- [x]' inside the AC block. Discovered in TASK-099 and confirmed again in TASK-103; several tasks were left with unticked ACs despite complete implementations.

The four-concern decomposition model for orchestration files: (1) pure resolution/mapping functions as leaf modules with no internal deps, (2) a factory/builder that composes the leaf modules into a configured object, (3) tool registration functions that take (pi, getRuntime) and encapsulate one tool each, (4) a thin entry-point (index.ts or agent-spawner.ts) that wires everything together and re-exports the public API. Future extensions that grow too large should follow this same four-layer split.
